prevalence.trend: stable; improved diagnosis with immunology referral and flow cytometry

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare) but high preventable burden without immunoglobulin replacement
burden.mortalityRate: Low with adequate Ig replacement; higher with delayed diagnosis/recurrent severe infections

vulnerablePopulations: male infants after maternal Ig wanes (3-12 months)
patients with delayed referral to immunology
patients in regions with limited access to IVIG/SCIG
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Recurrent bacterial infections; markedly low IgG/IgA/IgM; absent/very low CD19+ B cells on flow cytometry; confirmatory BTK genetic testing

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; diagnosis via recurrent infections and immunology work-up
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good with regular IVIG/SCIG and infection prevention; variable due to supply and access

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Immunology referral for quantitative immunoglobulins and flow cytometry; genetic confirmation often centralized
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG/SCIG), prompt antibiotics, vaccination strategy (avoid live vaccines in severe antibody deficiency), bronchiectasis monitoring
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Geographic inequity in immunology specialists and consistent access to immunoglobulin products
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: When available, IVIG programs markedly reduce infections and hospitalizations
